hinge: Cast bridge distortion occurs when seating or occlusal forces exceed the metal's elastic limit, causing plastic deformation so the framework no longer returns to its intended fit. Occlusal discrepancy alone does not permanently warp a casting without exceeding the elastic limit.

Amalgam marginal ditching deeper than 0.5 mm indicates breakdown with possible recurrent caries underneath. Patching the ditch with composite over old amalgam creates an unreliable interface. Full replacement is the appropriate management when the defect exceeds this threshold.

Multiple divergent implants need an open-tray impression technique with two-piece transfer copings to capture true implant angulation without interference from insertion path. Closed-tray transfers fail when implant paths of draw diverge.

In lateral excursion, posterior group-function contacts (especially heavy balancing and working posterior loading) are more destructive to the periodontium than mutually protected canine guidance with posterior disclusion. Shallow cuspal inclines and canine guidance reduce lateral posterior stress.

For amalgam core build-ups in endodontically treated teeth, self-threading pins provide the greatest retention in dentin among classic pin types. Friction-lock and cemented pins have lower retention values.

Decreased freeway space means the interocclusal rest distance is insufficient — the denture teeth are too close together at rest position, indicating OVD is too high. Selective grinding of denture teeth in the lab reduces occlusal height and restores adequate freeway space. Making a new denture is reserved for cases where grinding alone cannot restore proper OVD.

Whole saliva contains electrolytes (potassium), secretory IgA antibodies, and lysozyme antimicrobial protein. Broad proteolytic enzyme activity is not a normal dominant salivary component compared to these established constituents.

A broken shallow amalgam in a small cavity is best replaced with bonded composite resin for conservative tooth-colored repair with adhesive retention. GIC and RMGI are weaker for stress-bearing areas; full amalgam replacement is more aggressive than needed.

In a high-caries pediatric patient, filled resin (flowable composite) sealants offer better wear resistance than unfilled resin while still penetrating fissures when isolation is adequate. GIC is an alternative under moisture risk but filled resin is the keyed choice for durability.

An adolescent deep bite can be reduced with a removable appliance incorporating an anterior bite plane that allows posterior eruption and intrusion/retroclination of incisors. Fixed appliances alone are not the only option; the removable anterior bite plane is specifically indicated for deep bite correction.

Uncontrolled diabetes (HbA1c ~10) with acute apical abscess and urgent extraction carries elevated infection risk. Postoperative antibiotics are indicated after source control to manage systemic infection risk, not just a single preoperative prophylactic dose.

Crown lengthening when keratinized gingiva is inadequate requires an apically repositioned flap with osseous recontouring to expose tooth structure while preserving or relocating the attached gingiva band. Simple gingivectomy removes keratinized tissue and is contraindicated in this scenario.

A radial land on rotary endodontic instruments provides a wider peripheral contact that reduces aggressive dentin engagement and taper lock, increasing safety by reducing transportation risk and fracture potential. It does not primarily increase cutting efficiency.

An MOD cavity extending to the mesiobuccal cusp tip means that cusp is undermined and requires coverage. A ceramic onlay covers cusps for protection; a ceramic inlay stays within the cuspal inclines and is insufficient when the cusp tip itself is involved.

History of myocardial infarction more than 60 days ago without ongoing acute issues places the patient in ASA III (severe systemic disease with functional limitation). ASA II is mild disease; ASA IV represents constant threat to life.

Coronal microleakage through a deficient temporary or permanent restoration is a major cause of endodontic failure. The marginal integrity of the temporary coronal seal strongly determines short- and long-term success, more so than sealer brand or obturation technique alone.
